---
title: "Error Messages"
description: "Read tracebacks and recognize common beginner error patterns."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Read tracebacks and recognize common beginner error patterns.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Error Messages** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Reading Tracebacks** — Use Python error messages to find the error type, file, line, and clue text.
- Section 2: **Common Errors** — Recognize frequent beginner errors and the first debugging question for each.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A traceback is easier to read when you use the same order every time: bottom
clue, location, suspicious expression, small experiment.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  red["Python prints red traceback"] --> bottom["Read the bottom line<br/>error type + message"]
  bottom --> location["Find file and line number"]
  location --> code["Inspect the expression on that line"]
  code --> values["Check names, values, and types"]
  values --> experiment["Change one thing and run again"]
```

Do not start by rewriting the whole program. Start by extracting one clue.
:::

## Section 1: Reading Tracebacks

Use Python error messages to find the error type, file, line, and clue text.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A traceback is like a GPS route to the crash site: start at the bottom for the error
type, then follow the listed locations.
:::

### Mental model

A traceback is a stack of clues showing where Python was when it failed.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
print(numbers[5])
```

### Walkthrough

1. `numbers = [1, 2, 3]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print(numbers[5])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Do not read only the first red line. The last line usually names the error type and the
main clue.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "debugging-reading-tracebacks",
  "title": "Reading Tracebacks checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Reading Tracebacks?",
      "options": [
        "A traceback is a stack of clues showing where Python was when it failed.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A traceback is a stack of clues showing where Python was when it failed."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A traceback is like a GPS route to the crash site: start at the bottom for the error type, then follow the listed locations."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Common Errors

Recognize frequent beginner errors and the first debugging question for each.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Common errors are like common road signs: once you know the sign, you know what to check
first.
:::

### Mental model

Common errors have recognizable patterns, such as NameError, TypeError, IndexError, and
SyntaxError.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
total = "2" + 3
```

### Walkthrough

1. `total = "2" + 3` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Different error types need different questions. A TypeError is not fixed the same way as
a NameError.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "debugging-common-errors",
  "title": "Common Errors checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Common Errors?",
      "options": [
        "Common errors have recognizable patterns, such as NameError, TypeError, IndexError, and SyntaxError.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Common errors have recognizable patterns, such as NameError, TypeError, IndexError, and SyntaxError."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Common errors are like common road signs: once you know the sign, you know what to check first."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Reading Tracebacks**: A traceback is a stack of clues showing where Python was when it failed.
- **Common Errors**: Common errors have recognizable patterns, such as NameError, TypeError, IndexError, and
SyntaxError.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python errors tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/errors.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
